content:
10 min read
Just now

--

GenAI Monday | AI Beyond the Hype
Press enter or click to view image in full size

One interview question almost made me cry 12 years ago.
I’m not even exaggerating. It wasn’t a coding question or a system design interview. And no it wasn’t one of those impossible brain teasers either. It was this:
“What happens when you type www.google.com into your browser and press Enter?”
I was interviewing at “XXXXX”. Easy, I thought.
So I started, you know the usual answer. The browser checks its cache first.
“What’s the cache? Why is it checking there first? What’s in it?”
Okay. Then it checks the OS cache. Then the hosts file.
“Why does a hosts file even exist? Who still uses it?”
Then it goes out for DNS resolution.
“What is DNS? What’s a resolver? What’s the difference between a recursive resolver and an authoritative server? What’s actually being returned?”
Then the browser has an IP and opens a TCP connection.
“What’s TCP? Walk me through the three-way handshake. What’s a SYN? What’s a SYN-ACK? What happens if the ACK never arrives?”
Then TLS.
“What’s being negotiated? What is the certificate proving? What is the client verifying?”
Then the HTTP request goes out.
“What’s in the header? What method? Why does it matter?”
Then the server responds.
“What is the server actually doing while you wait? What is Google executing on their end? Where is the CDN in this picture? Which node is serving you and why that one?”
Then the browser starts rendering.
“When does rendering begin? Before or after the full response arrives? What’s blocking? What isn’t?”
Every single answer I gave became a new question. There was no node on that entire stack he did not stop and dig into. Cache. Hosts file. DNS. TCP. TLS. HTTP. Server. CDN. Render.
Every one. Downnnnnnnnnnnnnn to the bone.
What I thought would be a five minute conversation turned into 45 minutes of me explaining what happens after typing one URL into one browser.
I walked out of that room exhausted. Sweating. And yes, I cried afterwards. 😭
I cried because there was no bottom. Every time I thought I had hit the floor, he lifted another panel and there was more machinery underneath. Something I did a multiple times a day without a single conscious thought turned out to be an entire civilization of protocols, all of it running silently, all of it load-bearing and I was being asked to account for every piece of it out loud with someone watching.
Looking back, that question was a gift( for the purpose of positiveity lol). It taught me something I have never forgotten.
The simplest actions in technology have the most complicated systems hiding underneath them. And almost nobody bothers to look.
Which brings me to the version of that question I would ask today.
“What happens when you type a prompt into ChatGPT and press Enter?”
Millions of people do this every single day. Almost none of them can tell you what happens in the two seconds that follow.
Every answer became another question. Every layer revealed another layer.
That is exactly what we are about to do here.
So let’s walk the stack.

Here is the whole thing on one page. Then we take it apart.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Five stages. Roughly two seconds.

Your words stop being words
The first thing that happens has nothing to do with language.
Your text gets chopped into tokens.
A token is not a word. It is a chunk, sometimes a full word, sometimes a fragment, sometimes just a space or a comma. “Governance” might be one token. “Hallucination” might be three. The word “ChatGPT” splits differently than you would expect.
From this point on, the model is not reasoning over letters the way you and I do. It is reasoning over tokens. A list of numbers, each one pointing to a chunk in a vocabulary it learned during training.
This is why AI is famously bad at counting the letters in a word. It is not being stupid. It is not operating at the level of letters at all. You are asking it to count something it does not really perceive.
Okay. So the numbers go into the model. Then what?
The numbers get a location
hmnn Location? Yup! Each token ID gets converted into an embedding.
An embedding is a long list of numbers, often over a thousand of them, that places that token somewhere in a very high dimensional space of meaning.
Here is the useful way to think about it okay? Tokens that show up in similar contexts end up living near each other in that space. “Invoice” and “receipt” are neighbors. “Firewall” and “perimeter” are neighbors. “Bank” is a problem, because it lives in two neighborhoods at once, and the model has to figure out which one you meant.
Which is exactly what happens next.
Fine Tola! But how does it figure out which neighborhood?
Everything looks at everything else
This is the part people either love or glaze over at, so I am going to make it concrete.
Say you type: the bank by the river.
The model has to work out that you mean a riverbank, not a financial institution. It does this using a mechanism called attention. Every token in your prompt looks at every other token simultaneously and calculates how relevant each one is to it. “Bank” looks at “river” and adjusts. “River” looks at “bank” and adjusts. The meaning gets negotiated across the whole sentence at once.
Now stack that. Not once. Dozens of layers, one on top of the other, each one refining the representation a little further.
By the time your prompt has passed through every layer, the model has built a dense internal picture of what you asked, what you meant, and what surrounds it.
And none of this is happening on your laptop.
Behind the scenes, thousands of GPUs are running these calculations in parallel across a data center. That is the actual reason you get an answer in seconds instead of hours. When people talk about the compute crunch, the chip shortage, the energy footprint of AI, this is the step they are talking about. This one. Right here.
Now here is the part that surprised me when I first learned it.
It still has not generated a single word.
At this point, it is still trying to understand your prompt.
So when does it actually write something you may ask?
It is guessing. One word at a time.
This is the part that surprises almost everyone, including people who work in tech.
The model does not think of an answer and then type it out for you.
It predicts the next token. Then it looks at everything again, including the token it just produced, and predicts the next one. Then it does it again. And again. Until it decides to stop.
That is it. That is the whole thing. It is running a probability distribution across tens of thousands of possible next tokens and picking one, over and over, at speed.
Which means temperature suddenly makes sense.
Temperature controls how adventurous that pick is. Turn it down and the model almost always grabs the most likely next token, so you get output that is predictable, consistent, and a little boring. Turn it up and it starts reaching for less likely tokens, so you get output that is more creative, more surprising, and considerably more likely to be wrong.
Every single word you have ever read in a ChatGPT response was predicted. Not retrieved. Not looked up. PREDICTED! and yes everytime I type in caps, I’m yelling!
Okay sit with the serious stuff for a second.
And before someone asks: yes, this still holds for the reasoning models. GPT-5, o-series, Claude with extended thinking. They spend additional compute planning intermediate reasoning steps before they answer you. But underneath that planning, they are still generating token by token, the same way. The thinking is more elaborate. The mechanism has not changed.
so so so so how does it get to my screen?
It streams while it thinks
Each token gets decoded back into text and pushed to your screen the moment it exists.
That is why the response types itself out at you instead of appearing all at once. It is not a design flourish as you may have thought. You are actually watching the model work in real time.
It stops when it predicts a stop token, or when it hits the ceiling on output length. Whichever comes first.
And that is the stack.

Now here is why any of this matters
Once you understand the machine underneath, a lot of behavior that confuses people stops being mysterious.
Why does it make things up? Because it is not looking anything up. It is completing a pattern. Nothing in that pipeline checks whether the pattern happens to be true. A fabricated legal citation and a real one look identical to the model, because the model learned the shape of a citation, not the contents of a case database. There is no fact checker in the loop. There is only prediction. PREDICTION!!
Why does the same prompt give different answers? Because sampling introduces randomness by design. It was never deterministic. You just assumed it was.
Why does it forget? Because it can only attend to what is in the context window. It does not remember Tuesday. It does not know what you told it last session. If it is not in the window, it does not exist.
Why do longer, sharper prompts work better? Because you are feeding the attention mechanism more signal to work with. Vague prompts do not give it enough to attend to, so it fills the gap with whatever is statistically plausible. Which is a polite way of saying it guesses. I will not be diving into details here! I HAVE TAUGHT YOU FOLKS BETTER PROMPTING IN THE PAST!

But can you actually see any of this?
Here is where the analogy gets interesting.
When you are learning how the web works, DevTools is right there. Open Chrome, hit F12, load a page, and watch the whole thing happen. DNS, requests, headers, timing, the lot. The stack becomes visible.
So people( nerds) reasonably ask: is there a DevTools for AI?
Partially. And the honest answer is worth knowing.
For most commercial models, the transformer is a black box. The weights, the attention calculations, the internal representations, all of it runs server side and no vendor is opening it up for you. In the ChatGPT interface, you are sitting in the passenger seat with the hood welded shut.
But you have more visibility than you think, if you know where to look.
The tokenizer. OpenAI publishes one at platform.openai.com/tokenizer. Paste anything in and watch it split into tokens, color coded, right in front of you. Do this once and I promise it changes how you write prompts. You start seeing your own words the way the model does.
Logprobs. Go through the API instead of the chat window and you can ask for log probabilities on every token generated. Now you can see how confident the model was at each step. Was it 97 percent certain on that word, or was it essentially flipping a coin between three options? This is the closest thing that exists to watching it think. And here is the part that matters: the low confidence tokens are very often exactly where the hallucinations are hiding.
The Playground. platform.openai.com/playground. Move temperature, cap the tokens, change the system prompt, watch the output shift. It is not DevTools, but it is a control panel, and most people have never opened it.
Observability tooling. LangSmith. Langfuse. Helicone. If you are deploying AI inside a product or a workflow, these give you real traces. Full request and response chains, token counts, latency, cost per call. This is the actual DevTools equivalent, and it is where the engineering discipline lives.
I will say the uncomfortable part out loud. If your organization is running AI in production and cannot produce a trace of what the model was asked and what it returned, you do not have a tooling gap. You have a governance gap. And in a regulated industry, that gap has a name, and eventually it has a fine attached to it.

The question was never really about DNS
That interviewer was not testing whether I had memorized the OSI model.
He was testing whether I understood that everything simple has something complicated underneath it, and whether
